title: 帷幕
summary:
While reflecting on the past, the narrator observes that Styles has always been a "house of misery." He recalls that his friend John and his wife were miserable, Lawrence Cavendish was gloomy, Cynthia was frustrated by her lack of independence, and Ingersoll married a rich widow for money. The narrator tells Miss Cole that everyone is unhappy, including Judith. Miss Cole notes that Sir William is different because he is a successful man from the outside world rather than an invalid.

Miss Cole then reveals her true identity, explaining that "Richfield" is her mother's maiden name and she was formerly known as Matthew Richfield. She describes her childhood as being like a prison due to her temperamental father. She becomes emotional while discussing her sister, Maggie, who confessed to a crime and turned herself in to the police. The narrator privately believes that Maggie did not commit the crime, though he decides not to tell Miss Cole yet.

Later, around six o'clock, Colonel Luttrell (also referred to as Lethbridge or Letreil) arrives carrying a hunting rifle and dead pigeons. He appears distracted and out of sorts. As the group walks toward the house, they see Mr. Norton and Boyd Carrington on the veranda. Colonel Luttrell offers to buy the men drinks, but as he goes inside to fetch a bottle, his wife, Daisy, interrupts him. She angrily berates him for his lack of business sense and his habit of giving drinks away, eventually seizing the wine bottle and locking it in a cabinet. After Daisy storms out, a visibly aged and upset Colonel Luttrell returns to the group, claiming the whiskey is out. The encounter leaves the group, including Norton and Carrington, feeling stunned and awkward.
